posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: This was out of control. Absolutely delicious. Detailed notes later.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: You’re touching on some top secret info…2013 made some good whites.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked: White or red?

posts.name: Emmanuel Menger
posts.cooked: Clos de la Boudriotte is a red. Boudriotte is a white

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Emmanuel Menger:

Clos de la Boudriotte is a red. Boudriotte is a white

Hence my query, as above a white was mentioned…

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: It was the red.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: This had a beautiful nose that suggested Chambolle or Volnay, with a bit of earth and sous bois. Lots of red fruits, and great palate presence. It wasn’t a huge wine by any means and I’m not sure how well it’d age, but had surprising complexity and was remarkably well integrated. We drank it along with some pretty heavy hitters and it more than held its own.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: Gorgeous stuff. The Clos St Jean was also incredibly delicious from 2013.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I grabbed some 14 recently; we’ll see how it is, slightly better regarded vintage.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: Michael. The 14s from Ramonet are exquisite. Much better than 2013 to me. Enjoy! The 2014 Clos St Jean was one of my favorite wines even over many from Cotes de Nuits.

posts.name: Jonathan H
posts.cooked:
dcornutt:

That is great to hear! I just picked up some of that exact wine. Any comments on the '14 Clos de la Boudriette and village Chassagne-Montrachet red and white?

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I got some of the 15 CSJ and CDB as well which I imagine will also be very good.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: There are still a few deals on the 13s of these, it seems like the 15s are quite a bit more expensive.

posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked: I drank the 1966 at the weekend. Ethereal, delicate old Burgundy.

posts.name: Charlie Gierling
posts.cooked: Are “Clos de la Boudriotte” and “La Boudriotte” 2 different vineyards?

posts.name: Subu Ramachandran
posts.cooked: Nice post Michael. Ramonet is one of the few estates where both reds and whites are top quality. Glad to hear 2013s are showing well. I have a bottle or two to check later.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Are “Clos de la Boudriotte” and “La Boudriotte” 2 different vineyards?

Ramonet’s white Boudriotte actually comes from the lieu-dit of Fairendes which is one of several lieux-dits within Morgeot that has the right to be sold as Boudriotte.

The Clos de la Boudriotte is a clos within the lieu-dit of Boudriotte that Pierre Ramonet used to call his “little Romanée-Conti”. It was one of the first vineyards he acquired. I would personally rank the domaine’s Clos Saint-Jean on the same level or a step above.

posts.name: J0hn $mith
posts.cooked:
Ramonet’s white Boudriotte actually comes from the lieu-dit of Fairendes which is one of several lieux-dits within Morgeot that has the right to be sold as Boudriotte.

The Clos de la Boudriotte is a clos within the lieu-dit of Boudriotte that Pierre Ramonet used to call his “little Romanée-Conti”. It was one of the first vineyards he acquired. I would personally rank the domaine’s Clos Saint-Jean on the same level or a step above.

William, thanks for the note!

posts.name: Charlie Gierling
posts.cooked:
“Ramonet’s white Boudriotte actually comes from the lieu-dit of Fairendes which is one of several lieux-dits within Morgeot that has the right to be sold as Boudriotte.”
Strange. There is a lieu-dit “Les Fairandes”, but not as Part of Morgeot: Weinlagen

posts.name: Charlie Gierling
posts.cooked:
The Clos de la Boudriotte is a clos within the lieu-dit of Boudriotte that Pierre Ramonet used to call his “little Romanée-Conti”. It was one of the first vineyards he acquired. I would personally rank the domaine’s Clos Saint-Jean on the same level or a step above.

Do you know wich part of Boudriotte ?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
“Ramonet’s white Boudriotte actually comes from the lieu-dit of Fairendes which is one of several lieux-dits within Morgeot that has the right to be sold as Boudriotte.”
Strange. There is a lieu-dit “Les Fairandes”, but not as Part of Morgeot: Weinlagen

Fairendes can be sold as Morgeot and I’m pretty sure it’s technically part of Morgeot - like Tête du Clos and others - but would need to look it up in Pitiot, and I’m traveling right now.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: What’s the difference between the reds labeled 1er clos de la Boudriette and the reds labeled 1er Boudriette?

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
“Ramonet’s white Boudriotte actually comes from the lieu-dit of Fairendes which is one of several lieux-dits within Morgeot that has the right to be sold as Boudriotte.”
Strange. There is a lieu-dit “Les Fairandes”, but not as Part of Morgeot: Weinlagen

Fairendes can be sold as Morgeot and I’m pretty sure it’s technically part of Morgeot - like Tête du Clos and others - but would need to look it up in Pitiot, and I’m traveling right now.

My understanding is that Tete du Clos, Les Petits Clos, Les Grands Clos and Les Fairendes are all part of Morgeot. I don’t have a lot of experience with the former three, but Jean-Marc Pillot makes a lovely Morgeot-Fairendes Blanc, always among the best to my palate in his wide range. It seems to benefit from its northern position.

Michael, you nailed the 2013 Ramonet Clos de la Boudriotte. It is a lovely, fine, soil-driven, red-fruited, lissome wine. Like the domaine’s whites, all of the reds are superb but, for my tastes, the Clos de la Boudriotte merits the small premium it carries. William, I’d be interested to hear how you compare/contrast the domaine’s Clos de la Boudriotte and Clos St. Jean. I find that the latter has a shade more concentration/density, but not quite the fine texture and finesse.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: I have two bottles of 1992 Ramonet Clos De la Boudriotte. Now, if I could just find them…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
William, I’d be interested to hear how you compare/contrast the domaine’s Clos de la Boudriotte and Clos St. Jean. I find that the latter has a shade more concentration/density, but not quite the fine texture and finesse.

That’s pretty much exactly what I would have written, adding that the Clos Saint-Jean can age better, and benefits from the additional mid-palate flesh in leaner vintages such as 1993. I buy both very happily.
